1: The historical anchor: the year Sargon of Assyria sent his commander-in-chief to attack Ashdod. The specific historical detail grounds the sign-act of vv 2-6 in a documented event (Sargon's campaign of 711 BCE). The prophet's body becomes the medium of revelation in the most visceral way: Isaiah's physical condition over three years is the message YHWH communicates to Israel. The NT equivalent: the incarnation itself is the ultimate sign-act in which the divine being takes on the conditions of human vulnerability to deliver a message that no verbal oracle could match.
2: YHWH speaks through Isaiah: go, remove the sackcloth and take off your sandals. Isaiah obeyed — he walked naked and barefoot. The sackcloth that Isaiah removes was probably his normal prophetic garb (cf. Zechariah 13:4). The nakedness and barefoot-ness are the marks of captivity and poverty — the condition of prisoners taken in war. By wearing these conditions for three years, Isaiah embodies the fate that awaits those who trust Egypt and Cush. The NT sign-act equivalent: Jesus' own body is the sign — the teacher who had nowhere to lay his head (Matthew 8:20), stripped at Golgotha (John 19:23), is the living embodiment of what judgment looks like when it falls on the one who takes it on behalf of others.
3: YHWH interprets: just as Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign and portent against Egypt and Cush. The three years are deliberate: not a brief symbolic action but a sustained embodiment. The sign (ot) and portent (mofet) are the two terms used for the plagues of Egypt (Exodus 7:3) — the prophetic act recapitulates the exodus judgment, now applied to Egypt itself. Isaiah's body is a living prophecy: the prophet who speaks of the Servant's suffering (Isaiah 52:13-53:12) first enacts a lesser version of stripping and humiliation.
4: The king of Assyria will lead away Egyptian and Cushite captives naked and barefoot, with buttocks uncovered — the shame of Egypt exposed. The explicit shameful exposure of the captives is the judgment that Isaiah's three years of symbolic nakedness has been announcing. In the Christological pattern: the Servant is stripped and shamed in a way that should belong to others (Isaiah 52:14; Isaiah 53:3-4) — his exposure is substitutionary, taking on what the rebels deserve. Isaiah's years of nakedness are a prophetic type of the Servant's ultimate stripping.
5: Those who looked to Cush with expectation and took pride in Egypt will be terrified and put to shame. The political trust in Egypt — the alliance that seemed pragmatically wise — is the precise thing condemned. The shame is proportional to the hope: the greater the expectation placed in Egypt, the greater the humiliation when Egypt falls. In the NT, the same logic applies to any trust placed in human strength: those who make flesh their strength (Isaiah 17:5) will be ashamed when the arm of flesh fails.
6: In that day the coastland people will say: This is what happened to those we relied on, to whom we fled for help and rescue from the king of Assyria — how will we escape? The confession of the coastlands is the universal admission that human political alliances cannot save. The three-year sign-act concludes with this admission. The NT ground: blessed are those who trust in YHWH (Jeremiah 17:7); cursed is the one who trusts in man (Jeremiah 17:5-6). The coastlands' question — how will we escape? — is the question that the gospel answers: not by any political alliance but by the one who has already faced the Assyrian-equivalent and overcome (cf. Isaiah 37:36).
